summary: Luu argues against generic "learn what I learned" career advice, advocating instead for identifying a few skills that match your personal aptitudes and learning them in environments with world-class experts. Rather than being a broad generalist or following popular recommendations (like learning Haskell or compilers), he suggests focusing on amplifying strengths through deliberate practice in conducive environments. He uses his own experience with skills like "having an adversarial mindset" and "looking at data" to illustrate how specialization and good learning environments trump breadth or fashionable technical choices.
key_points: Most career advice amounts to "learn what I learned" without considering individual aptitudes or contexts, making it generally useless
Specialization and amplifying strengths (the "few tricks" approach) is often more effective than being a broad generalist
The best skills to learn are those you have a relative aptitude for and can't help but apply, not what's trendy or what worked for others
Learning environments matter enormously - working with world-class experts accelerates skill development far more than books or internet advice
Finding suitable skills often requires trial and error rather than having a predetermined long-term career strategy
money_quotes: When people said things like 'Forth is very productive', what I suspect they really meant was 'Forth makes me very productive and I have not considered how well this generalizes to people with different aptitudes or who are operating in different contexts'.

I think that people who are really good at something have too many bits of information in their head about how to do it for that information to really be compressible into a book, let alone a blog post.

A random walk will only move you sqrt(n) in some direction whereas a directed walk will move you n in some direction... but I think that, for many people, that advice implicitly underestimates the difficulty of finding an area that's suited to you.
Look for things you can't help but do that most other people don't seem to do.
The environment, how you spend your day-to-day, is inherently local.
key_insight: Effective skill development comes from identifying your natural aptitudes through trial and error, then deliberately practicing those specific skills in environments surrounded by world-class experts—not from following generic advice about what technologies or fields to learn.
